narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, such as your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.50, which stands above the typical student's 3.21. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of failing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 69% chance of failure for you, leading to the rejection of your application to protect both you and the school's outcomes. This prediction was driven primarily by your academic decile rankings from school—specifically, your Year 3 decile of 1, which was the strongest factor pushing the model toward expecting a bar failure, followed closely by your similar Year 1 decile of 1, which also significantly contributed in that direction. While your LSAT score of 27.50, which is below the average of 36.70, had a minor influence toward failure, it wasn't a top driver. On a more positive note, your above-average undergrad GPA worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass, as did your low family income and your plan to attend full-time, like the vast majority of students (over 92%), both of which helped counterbalance some risks. Compared to typical applicants, your early academic deciles are much lower than the averages around 5.5, which weighed heavily against you, even though your GPA shines brighter than most. This isn't a reflection of your potential as a Black woman breaking barriers—it's the model highlighting patterns from past students where low early deciles often correlated with bar struggles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to or above the 36.70 average, strengthening your high school decile performance if possible through additional coursework or programs, and exploring scholarships or support tailored for women and Black students in law to address income-related hurdles. Reapply after addressing these key areas, perhaps with a part-time option if it fits better, and seek mentorship from successful Black lawyers who've navigated similar paths. You've already outperformed averages in GPA and shown resilience—building on that can shift your prediction dramatically. We're rooting for you to succeed.
